Q10.title: Pharmaceutical Quality System
Q10.summary: ICH Q10 describes a comprehensive model for an effective pharmaceutical quality system (PQS) based on ISO quality concepts, incorporating GMP requirements and complementing [[wiki/guidelines/Q8-R2]] and [[wiki/guidelines/Q9-R1]]. It covers the entire product lifecycle: development, technology transfer, commercial manufacturing, and product discontinuation. It is the overarching quality management framework for the pharmaceutical industry.
Q10.scope: Applies to systems supporting development and manufacture of drug substances (APIs) and drug products, including biotech/biological products, throughout the entire product lifecycle. Content additional to current regional GMP requirements is optional.
Q10.key_requirements:
### Three Objectives (Section 1.5)
1. Achieve product realisation — deliver products with appropriate quality attributes.
2. Establish and maintain a state of control — effective monitoring and control of process performance and product quality.
3. Facilitate continual improvement — identify and implement quality, process, and variability reduction improvements.

### Two Enablers (Section 1.6)
4. **Knowledge Management**: Systematic approach to acquiring, analysing, storing, and disseminating product/process/component information. Sources: prior knowledge, development studies, technology transfer, process validation, manufacturing experience, innovation, change management.
5. **Quality Risk Management**: Integral to PQS. Proactive approach per [[wiki/guidelines/Q9-R1]].

### Quality Manual (Section 1.8)
6. Required. Must contain: quality policy, scope, identification of PQS processes with sequences/linkages, management responsibilities.

### Management Responsibility (Section 2)
7. Senior management has ultimate responsibility for PQS.
8. Must participate in design, implementation, monitoring, maintenance of PQS.
9. Quality policy established, communicated, periodically reviewed.
10. Quality objectives defined, supported by all levels, aligned with strategy.
11. Adequate resources (human, financial, materials, facilities, equipment) provided.
12. Internal communication processes ensuring escalation of quality issues.
13. Management review of PQS for continuing suitability and effectiveness.
14. Outsourced activities and purchased materials: assessed, defined in agreements, monitored, incoming materials verified from approved sources.
15. Product ownership changes: responsibilities defined, information transferred.

### Four PQS Elements (Section 3.2)

**3.2.1 Process Performance and Product Quality Monitoring**
16. Use QRM to establish control strategy.
17. Measurement tools (data management, statistical tools) for parameters/attributes.
18. Verify continued state of control.
19. Identify variation sources for continual improvement.
20. Include internal and external feedback (complaints, rejections, deviations, audits, inspections).

**3.2.2 CAPA System**
21. Implement corrective and preventive actions from investigations of complaints, rejections, non-conformances, recalls, deviations, audits, inspections, trends.
22. Structured approach to investigation; determine root cause.
23. Level of effort/formality commensurate with risk per [[wiki/guidelines/Q9-R1]].

**3.2.3 Change Management System**
24. Evaluate proposed changes using QRM.
25. Evaluate against marketing authorisation and design space.
26. Expert teams with appropriate expertise review changes.
27. Post-implementation evaluation to confirm objectives met and no deleterious impact.

**3.2.4 Management Review of Process Performance and Product Quality**
28. Review inputs: inspection results, periodic quality reviews (customer satisfaction, complaints, recalls), monitoring conclusions, CAPA effectiveness, changes.
29. Actions: improvements, resources/training, knowledge capture and dissemination.

### Continual Improvement of PQS (Section 4)
30. Formal periodic review of PQS.
31. Performance indicators: complaint/deviation/CAPA/change processes, outsourced activity feedback, self-assessments, external assessments.
32. Monitor internal/external factors: emerging regulations, innovations, business changes, product ownership changes.

### Regulatory Opportunities (Annex 1)
33. Four scenarios with increasing levels of PQS and product understanding, each offering greater regulatory flexibility (risk-based inspections, science-based assessment, optimized post-approval changes, real-time release).
Q10.definitions:
- **Pharmaceutical Quality System (PQS)**: Management system to direct and control a pharmaceutical company with regard to quality.
- **State of Control**: Condition in which set of controls consistently provides assurance of continued process performance and product quality.
- **Control Strategy**: Planned set of controls derived from product and process understanding.
- **Knowledge Management**: Systematic approach to acquiring, analysing, storing, disseminating product/process/component information.
- **Product Realisation**: Achievement of a product with appropriate quality attributes meeting patient, HCP, regulatory, and internal needs.
- **Continual Improvement**: Recurring activity to increase ability to fulfil requirements.
- **CAPA**: Corrective action (prevent recurrence) + preventive action (prevent occurrence).
- **Change Management**: Systematic approach to proposing, evaluating, approving, implementing, reviewing changes.
- **Design Space**: Multidimensional combination of input variables and process parameters demonstrated to provide quality assurance. Working within design space is not a regulatory change.
Q10.contradictions:
- Q10 explicitly states content beyond current GMP is optional — implementation incentivized by potential regulatory flexibility but not mandated.
- "State of control" is defined but quantitative criteria for assessing it are left to the company — this leads to varied interpretation across industry.
- The relationship between Q10's change management system and regional post-approval change requirements (e.g., SUPAC, EU variations) is referenced but not fully harmonized.

Q11.title: Development and Manufacture of Drug Substances (Chemical Entities and Biotechnological/Biological Entities)
Q11.summary: ICH Q11 provides guidance for the development and manufacture of drug substances, both chemical and biotechnological. It extends [[wiki/guidelines/Q8-R2]] principles to drug substance manufacturing, covering process development, control strategy, and process validation.
Q11.scope: Chemical entities and biotechnological/biological entities used as drug substances.
Q11.key_requirements:
1. Manufacturing process development and description.
2. Selection and justification of starting materials and source materials.
3. Control strategy for drug substance: material attributes, process parameters, in-process controls, specifications.
4. Process validation and/or evaluation approach.
5. API starting material designation and justification.
6. Design space applicability to drug substance manufacturing.
7. Lifecycle management approach.
Q11.contradictions: - Relationship between Q7 GMP requirements and Q11 enhanced development approach can create complexity in defining the starting point for GMP.

Q12.title: Technical and Regulatory Considerations for Pharmaceutical Product Lifecycle Management
Q12.summary: ICH Q12 provides a framework for managing post-approval changes to chemistry, manufacturing, and controls (CMC) throughout the product lifecycle. It introduces concepts of Established Conditions (ECs), Post-Approval Change Management Protocols (PACMPs), and product lifecycle management (PLCM) tools to enable innovation and continual improvement.
Q12.scope: Applies to commercially approved drug substances and drug products throughout their lifecycle. Both chemical and biological/biotechnological products.
Q12.key_requirements:
1. **Established Conditions (ECs)**: Legally binding information in the approved dossier — changes to ECs require regulatory notification/approval. Defined elements include process parameters, material attributes, equipment, and analytical procedures that impact CQAs.
2. **Categorization of post-approval changes**: Based on risk and impact on quality. Different reporting categories (e.g., prior approval, notification, annual report).
3. **Post-Approval Change Management Protocol (PACMP)**: Pre-approved protocol for managing specific anticipated post-approval changes. If followed, may enable reduced reporting category.
4. **Product Lifecycle Management (PLCM)**: Systematic approach linking development knowledge, manufacturing experience, and regulatory requirements.
5. **Regulatory Assessment Enhancement**: Enhanced change categorization and tools to promote innovation in commercial manufacturing.
6. **Knowledge management**: Maintained throughout lifecycle to support change management decisions.
Q12.contradictions:
- EC concept is powerful but regional implementation varies significantly.
- PACMP adoption has been slow due to regulatory uncertainty.
- Relationship between Q12 tools and existing regional variation/supplement systems is complex.

Q13.title: Continuous Manufacturing of Drug Substances and Drug Products
Q13.summary: ICH Q13 provides guidance on the development, implementation, and lifecycle management of continuous manufacturing (CM) for drug substances and drug products. It addresses scientific and regulatory considerations unique to CM, including batch definition, process dynamics, control strategy, and real-time release testing.
Q13.scope: Continuous manufacturing of small molecule drug substances and drug products, including integrated continuous processes and hybrid (batch + continuous) approaches. Scientific principles also applicable to biotech, though specific biotech CM guidance may vary.
Q13.key_requirements:
1. **Batch definition**: In CM, a batch can be defined by quantity produced or by time of operation. Must be clearly defined and documented.
2. **Process dynamics**: Residence time distribution (RTD), start-up/shutdown procedures, and transient behavior must be understood and controlled.
3. **Material traceability and diversion**: System to trace materials through the process and divert non-conforming material in real time.
4. **Control strategy**: Enhanced process monitoring and control. PAT (Process Analytical Technology) typically integral. Real-time monitoring of CQAs.
5. **Real-time release testing (RTRT)**: CM particularly suited for RTRT per enhanced control strategy.
6. **Process validation**: Approach adapted for CM — traditional 3-batch validation may not apply. Continued process verification emphasized.
7. **Equipment design**: Integrated process trains, modular equipment, cleaning considerations for continuous operation.
8. **Stability**: Standard [[wiki/guidelines/Q1A]] approach applies. Batch for stability defined consistently with batch definition for CM.
9. **Scale considerations**: Scale-up in CM often involves running longer rather than using larger equipment.
10. **Lifecycle management**: Per [[wiki/guidelines/Q12]] principles.
Q13.contradictions:
- Batch definition in CM differs fundamentally from traditional manufacturing — regulatory alignment still evolving.
- RTRT is enabled but acceptance varies by region.
- Cleaning validation for continuous operation without batch-to-batch cleaning requires novel approaches.

Q14.title: Analytical Procedure Development
Q14.summary: ICH Q14 provides guidance on the development of analytical procedures. It complements [[wiki/guidelines/Q2-R2]] (validation) by covering the development phase that precedes validation. Introduces concepts of Analytical Target Profile (ATP), enhanced analytical development, and analytical procedure lifecycle management.
Q14.scope: Analytical procedures used for release and stability testing of drug substances and drug products. Also applicable to analytical procedures in the broader control strategy.
Q14.key_requirements:
1. **Analytical Target Profile (ATP)**: Prospective description of analytical procedure performance requirements (analogous to QTPP in [[wiki/guidelines/Q8-R2]]).
2. **Minimal vs. enhanced approach**: Minimal approach follows traditional development. Enhanced approach uses systematic, science-based development with multivariate experiments, leading to deeper understanding.
3. **Knowledge management**: Prior knowledge, platform methods, and development data systematically managed.
4. **Analytical procedure design space**: Multidimensional space within which analytical procedure parameters can be varied without affecting reportable results. Analogous to process design space in Q8.
5. **Robustness**: Evaluated during development (not just validation). Results inform system suitability tests and method operable design region (MODR).
6. **System Suitability Test (SST)**: Established during development as ongoing check of performance. Integral part of procedure.
7. **Lifecycle management**: Post-approval changes to analytical procedures managed through established conditions and control strategy, linked to [[wiki/guidelines/Q12]].
8. **Continual improvement**: Analytical procedures can be improved post-approval within the analytical procedure design space or through regulatory change processes.
Q14.contradictions:
- Q14 was developed simultaneously with Q2(R2) — they are companion documents and should be read together.
- Enhanced analytical development approach adoption still maturing in industry.
- Analytical procedure design space concept acceptance varies by region.

Q1A.title: Stability Testing of New Drug Substances and Products
Q1A.summary: ICH Q1A(R2) defines the core stability data package required for registration of new drug substances and drug products in the EU, Japan, and USA. It establishes storage conditions, testing frequencies, batch requirements, evaluation methods, and labeling expectations for formal stability studies. This is the foundational ICH stability guideline — nearly all other Q1-series guidelines build on it.
Q1A.scope: Applies to new molecular entities and their associated drug products for registration applications. Does not cover abbreviated/abridged applications, variations, or clinical trial applications. Addresses climatic zones I and II. For new dosage forms, see [[wiki/guidelines/Q1C]]. For biotechnological/biological products, see [[wiki/guidelines/Q5C]].
Q1A.key_requirements:
### Drug Substance
1. Formal stability data required on at least 3 primary batches, manufactured at minimum pilot scale, using a synthetic route and process representative of production.
2. Studies conducted on drug substance in the same or simulated container closure system proposed for storage and distribution.
3. Stress testing on a single batch: temperature increments (50°C, 60°C, etc.), humidity (75% RH or greater), oxidation, photolysis, hydrolysis across wide pH range. Photostability per [[wiki/guidelines/Q1B]].
4. Testing frequency: every 3 months in year 1, every 6 months in year 2, annually thereafter.
5. Accelerated: minimum 3 time points over 6 months (0, 3, 6 months).
6. Intermediate (if triggered): minimum 4 time points over 12 months (0, 6, 9, 12 months).
7. Specifications per [[wiki/guidelines/Q6A]], [[wiki/guidelines/Q6B]]. Degradation products per [[wiki/guidelines/Q3A]].
8. Validated stability-indicating analytical procedures required.

### Storage Conditions — Drug Substance (General Case)
| Study | Condition | Minimum Duration |
|---|---|---|
| Long-term | 25°C ± 2°C / 60% RH ± 5% RH OR 30°C ± 2°C / 65% RH ± 5% RH | 12 months |
| Intermediate | 30°C ± 2°C / 65% RH ± 5% RH | 6 months |
| Accelerated | 40°C ± 2°C / 75% RH ± 5% RH | 6 months |

Note: If 30°C/65% RH is chosen as long-term, there is no intermediate condition.

### Storage Conditions — Refrigerated
| Study | Condition | Minimum Duration |
|---|---|---|
| Long-term | 5°C ± 3°C | 12 months |
| Accelerated | 25°C ± 2°C / 60% RH ± 5% RH | 6 months |

### Storage Conditions — Freezer
| Study | Condition | Minimum Duration |
|---|---|---|
| Long-term | -20°C ± 5°C | 12 months |

Below -20°C: case-by-case.

### Drug Product
9. Same batch, frequency, and storage requirements as drug substance, with additional considerations.
10. Photostability testing on at least one primary batch per [[wiki/guidelines/Q1B]].
11. Two of three primary batches at minimum pilot scale; third can be smaller if justified.
12. Stability testing after constitution/dilution if applicable.
13. Shelf life acceptance criteria may differ from release criteria if justified.
14. Antimicrobial preservative effectiveness testing on one primary batch at proposed shelf life.

### Significant Change — Drug Product
Defined as any of:
- 5% change in assay from initial value
- Any degradation product exceeding its acceptance criterion
- Failure to meet appearance, physical attributes, or functionality test criteria
- Failure to meet pH acceptance criterion
- Failure to meet dissolution for 12 dosage units

### Semi-Permeable Containers
15. Additional water loss evaluation required. Low RH conditions used.
16. Long-term: 25°C/40% RH or 30°C/35% RH. Accelerated: 40°C/NMT 25% RH.
17. 5% water loss from initial value after equivalent of 3 months at 40°C/NMT 25% RH = significant change.
18. Water loss rate ratio table provided for calculating loss at reference RH from alternative RH data.

### Evaluation
19. Statistical approach: 95% one-sided confidence limit for mean degradation curve intersecting acceptance criterion.
20. Batch combination: apply statistical tests (p > 0.25) to slopes and intercepts before combining.
21. Limited extrapolation beyond observed data is acceptable if justified.
22. Mass balance should be reviewed.

### Stability Commitment
23. If long-term data does not cover proposed re-test period/shelf life at approval, post-approval commitment required.
24. Three scenarios defined based on how many production batches have data at submission.

### Labeling
25. Storage statement required. Avoid "ambient conditions" or "room temperature."
26. Re-test date (drug substance) or expiration date (drug product) on container label.
Q1A.definitions:
- **Accelerated testing**: Exaggerated storage conditions to increase rate of degradation; used to assess longer-term effects and short-term excursions.
- **Bracketing**: Testing only extremes of design factors (strength, package size) assuming intermediates are represented. See [[wiki/guidelines/Q1D]].
- **Matrixing**: Testing a subset of all factor combinations at each time point. See [[wiki/guidelines/Q1D]].
- **Climatic zones**: Four zones (I-IV) based on W. Grimm classification. Q1A addresses zones I and II. Zone III/IV addressed by [[wiki/guidelines/Q1F]].
- **Mean kinetic temperature**: Single derived temperature giving equivalent thermal challenge; based on Arrhenius equation, per J.D. Haynes formula.
- **Significant change (drug substance)**: Failure to meet specification.
- **Significant change (drug product)**: See list under Key Requirements above.
- **Pilot scale batch**: Fully representative of production process. For solid oral: minimum 1/10th production scale or 100,000 units, whichever is larger.
- **Re-test period**: Period during which drug substance can be used if stored under defined conditions; can be re-tested and used multiple times.
- **Shelf life**: Time during which drug product remains within approved specification under defined storage.
- **Semi-permeable containers**: Allow passage of solvent (water) but prevent solute loss. Examples: LDPE bags, pouches, ampoules, bottles, vials.
- **Impermeable containers**: Permanent barrier to gases/solvents. Examples: sealed aluminum tubes, sealed glass ampoules.
Q1A.contradictions:
- The R2 revision changed intermediate storage condition from 30°C/60% RH to 30°C/65% RH (consequence of Q1F adoption).
- 30°C/35% RH added as alternative long-term condition for semi-permeable containers.
- Mid-stream switch from 60% RH to 65% RH intermediate was permitted with documentation.

Q1B.title: Stability Testing: Photostability Testing of New Drug Substances and Products
Q1B.summary: ICH Q1B is an annex to [[wiki/guidelines/Q1A]] that provides specific recommendations for photostability testing. It defines light sources, exposure conditions, and a systematic testing approach for both drug substances and drug products.
Q1B.scope: Applies to new molecular entities and associated drug products for registration applications. Does not cover photostability after administration (in-use conditions).
Q1B.key_requirements:
### Light Sources (Two Options)
1. **Option 1**: Any light source producing output similar to D65/ID65 emission standard (artificial daylight fluorescent, xenon, or metal halide lamp). Filter radiation below 320 nm if present.
2. **Option 2**: Same sample exposed to both: (a) cool white fluorescent lamp per ISO 10977, and (b) near UV fluorescent lamp 320-400 nm, max emission 350-370 nm.

### Exposure Conditions
3. Confirmatory studies: minimum 1.2 million lux hours overall illumination AND minimum 200 watt hours/square meter integrated near UV energy.
4. Validated chemical actinometric system (e.g., quinine actinometry) or calibrated radiometers/lux meters.
5. Dark controls (wrapped in aluminum foil) placed alongside samples to evaluate thermal contribution.

### Drug Substance Testing
6. Two parts: forced degradation testing (method development, pathway elucidation) and confirmatory testing (handling, packaging, labeling decisions).
7. Forced degradation: drug substance alone and/or in simple solutions/suspensions in chemically inert transparent containers.
8. Confirmatory: single batch normally sufficient. If equivocal, test up to 2 additional batches.
9. Solid drug substances: spread in dish, max 3 mm thickness, with transparent cover.
10. Liquids: chemically inert transparent containers.

### Drug Product Testing — Sequential Approach
11. Test fully exposed product first → if unacceptable change → test in immediate pack → if still unacceptable → test in marketing pack → if still unacceptable → redesign package or reformulate.
12. Decision flow chart provided in guideline.
13. For products in completely light-impenetrable containers (aluminum tubes, cans), only directly exposed testing needed.
14. In-use photostability testing may be appropriate for infusion liquids, dermal creams, etc.
Q1B.definitions:
- **Immediate (primary) pack**: Packaging in direct contact with drug substance/product, including label.
- **Marketing pack**: Immediate pack + secondary packaging (e.g., carton).
- **Forced degradation testing**: Deliberate degradation to evaluate photosensitivity for method development and pathway elucidation.
- **Confirmatory studies**: Standardized conditions to establish precautionary measures for manufacturing, formulation, and packaging.
Q1B.contradictions:
- The guideline provides two light source options but does not mandate one over the other, leading to variability in industry practice.
- "Acceptable change" is defined as "change within limits justified by the applicant" — subjective threshold.

Q1C.title: Stability Testing for New Dosage Forms
Q1C.summary: ICH Q1C is a brief annex to [[wiki/guidelines/Q1A]] addressing stability data requirements for new dosage forms by the owner of the original application, after the original submission for the drug substance and product has been made.
Q1C.scope: New dosage forms: different pharmaceutical product type containing the same active substance as an existing approved drug product. Examples: different administration route (oral to parenteral), new delivery system (IR tablet to MR tablet), different dosage form same route (capsule to tablet, solution to suspension).
Q1C.key_requirements:
1. Stability protocols should follow [[wiki/guidelines/Q1A]] in principle.
2. A reduced stability database at submission time may be acceptable in justified cases (e.g., 6 months accelerated + 6 months long-term from ongoing studies).
Q1C.definitions: - **New dosage form**: Different pharmaceutical product type containing the same active substance as an existing approved product.
Q1C.contradictions: - Very brief guideline — leaves considerable room for interpretation on what constitutes adequate justification for a reduced database.

Q1D.title: Bracketing and Matrixing Designs for Stability Testing of New Drug Substances and Products
Q1D.summary: ICH Q1D provides guidance on reduced stability study designs — bracketing and matrixing — that can reduce the total number of samples tested while still supporting valid retest period or shelf life estimation. It supplements [[wiki/guidelines/Q1A]].
Q1D.scope: Applicable to formal stability studies for drug products (and, with limitations, drug substances). Covers design factors like strength, container size, fill volume, and container closure system.
Q1D.key_requirements:
### Bracketing
1. Only samples on the extremes of certain design factors (strength, container size, fill) are tested at all time points. Intermediates assumed represented by extremes.
2. Must demonstrate selected samples are truly the extremes.
3. Applicable to: identical or closely related formulations, same container with varying size or fill.
4. If extremes show different stability, intermediates assigned the shelf life of the least stable extreme.

### Matrixing
5. A subset of the total number of samples is tested at each time point. Different subsets at different time points.
6. Design should be balanced so each factor combination is tested equally over the study duration.
7. All selected combinations tested at initial and final time points. Only fractions tested at intermediate time points.
8. At 12 months (or last pre-submission time point), all selected combinations tested.
9. Minimum 3 time points including initial for each combination through first 12 months.
10. One-half reduction and one-third reduction strategies defined with examples.

### Applicability
11. Matrixing applicable when supporting data indicate predictable stability and small variability.
12. Moderate variability: statistical justification required. Large variability: matrixing not appropriate.
13. Matrixing on time points only has similar power to full design for detecting factor differences.
14. Matrixing on factors reduces precision in shelf life estimation.

### Data Evaluation
15. Data from reduced designs treated same as full design data.
Q1D.definitions:
- **Bracketing**: Testing only extremes of design factors (defined in [[wiki/guidelines/Q1A]] glossary).
- **Matrixing**: Testing a selected subset of all factor combinations at each time point (defined in [[wiki/guidelines/Q1A]] glossary).
- **Design factors**: Variables (strength, container size, fill) evaluated for effect on stability.
Q1D.contradictions:
- Bracketing and matrixing together in one design requires "careful consideration and scientific justification."
- For drug substances, matrixing has limited utility and bracketing is generally not applicable.

Q1E.title: Evaluation of Stability Data
Q1E.summary: ICH Q1E provides detailed recommendations on how to use stability data generated per [[wiki/guidelines/Q1A]] to propose a retest period or shelf life. It defines when and how extrapolation beyond observed data is justified and provides statistical approaches for data analysis.
Q1E.scope: Applies to new molecular entities and associated drug products. Covers single- and multi-factor designs, full and reduced designs. Addresses room temperature, refrigerated, freezer, and below -20°C storage.
Q1E.key_requirements:
### Extrapolation Rules (Room Temperature Storage)

**No significant change at accelerated:**

1. **Little/no change + little/no variability** (long-term and accelerated): statistical analysis normally unnecessary. Extrapolation up to 2x period covered by long-term data, but not more than 12 months beyond.
2. **Change and/or variability observed**: statistical analysis of long-term data useful.
   - Data NOT amenable to statistics: extrapolate up to 1.5x, not more than 6 months beyond.
   - Data amenable but no analysis performed: same as above.
   - Data amenable AND analysis performed: up to 2x, not more than 12 months beyond (backed by statistical result and supporting data).

**Significant change at accelerated:**
3. If long-term data show little/no change: extrapolate up to 1.5x long-term period, not more than 6 months beyond. Intermediate data should confirm no significant change. If intermediate shows significant change, shelf life based on long-term data only with no extrapolation.

### Refrigerated Products
4. No significant change at accelerated: retest period/shelf life based on real-time data, with extrapolation principles above.
5. Significant change at accelerated within 3 months: shelf life based on long-term data only, no extrapolation.

### Freezer Products
6. Retest period/shelf life based on real-time data at long-term condition only.

### Statistical Approaches (Appendix B)
7. Zero-order kinetics assumed for most quantitative chemical attributes (assay, degradation products, preservative content).
8. Poolability testing: level of significance 0.25 for slopes and intercepts across batches.
9. If pooling appropriate: combined data used for single shelf life estimate.
10. If pooling not appropriate: shelf life based on shortest period for any individual batch.
11. 95% one-sided confidence limit for mean degradation curve intersection with acceptance criterion.
12. Regression analysis: linear, quadratic, or cubic function; arithmetic or logarithmic scale.

### General Principles
13. Each attribute assessed separately; overall retest period/shelf life should not exceed that predicted for any single attribute.
14. Products should be formulated to provide 100% label claim at release.
15. Extrapolation always verified by additional long-term data as soon as available — include time point at end of extrapolated period in commitment batch protocol.
Q1E.definitions: - **Extrapolation**: Using known data set to infer information about future data beyond the period covered by long-term stability data.
Q1E.contradictions:
- The extrapolation limits (2x/12 months, 1.5x/6 months) are prescriptive but the underlying statistical justification varies by case.
- Decision tree in Appendix A provides systematic guidance but does not cover all possible scenarios.

Q1F.title: Stability Testing of Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients and Finished Pharmaceutical Products (Climatic Zones III/IV)
Q1F.summary: Originally ICH Q1F, this guideline was withdrawn by ICH in 2006 but adopted and maintained by WHO. It provides stability testing conditions for climatic zones III (hot/dry) and IV (hot/humid), extending [[wiki/guidelines/Q1A]] which covers zones I and II only. The long-term storage condition for zone IVb is 30°C/75% RH.
Q1F.scope: Active pharmaceutical ingredients and finished pharmaceutical products intended for markets in climatic zones III and IV. Particularly relevant for countries in Africa, Asia, Latin America, Middle East.
Q1F.key_requirements:
1. **Zone IVb long-term condition**: 30°C ± 2°C / 75% RH ± 5% RH — the most demanding standard condition.
2. **Accelerated**: 40°C ± 2°C / 75% RH ± 5% RH (same as Q1A).
3. Climatic zone classification: Zone I (temperate), Zone II (subtropical/Mediterranean), Zone III (hot/dry), Zone IVa (hot/humid), Zone IVb (hot/very humid).
4. Testing protocols follow [[wiki/guidelines/Q1A]] principles with modified storage conditions.
5. Mutual recognition of stability data across zones when conditions are met.
Q1F.contradictions:
- ICH withdrew Q1F in 2006 but WHO maintains it. The R2 revision of Q1A incorporated some Q1F changes (30°C/65% RH intermediate condition).
- Zone IVb (30°C/75% RH) is more demanding than any Q1A long-term condition.

Q2-R2.title: Validation of Analytical Procedures
Q2-R2.summary: ICH Q2(R2) provides guidance on the selection and evaluation of validation tests for analytical procedures used in release and stability testing of commercial drug substances and products. R2 (2023) is a complete revision incorporating multivariate procedures, non-linear response models, lifecycle concepts, and alignment with [[wiki/guidelines/Q14]].
Q2-R2.scope: Applies to analytical procedures used for release and stability testing of commercial drug substances and drug products. Can also apply to analytical procedures in the control strategy per [[wiki/guidelines/Q10]]. Scientific principles applicable in phase-appropriate manner during clinical development. Covers: assay, potency, purity, impurity (quantitative or limit test), identity, and other quantitative/qualitative measurements.
Q2-R2.key_requirements:
### General Considerations (Section 2)
1. Validation objective: demonstrate analytical procedure is fit for intended purpose.
2. Validation protocol required before study; validation report summarizing results required after.
3. Experimental design should reflect number of replicates used in routine analysis.
4. Reference materials with documented identity, purity, or other characteristics should be used.
5. Performance characteristics can be considered simultaneously (specificity, accuracy, precision over reportable range).
6. Aligned with [[wiki/guidelines/Q14]] — knowledge from development aids validation study design.
7. System Suitability Test (SST) established during development, integral part of procedure.
8. Robustness typically evaluated during development per [[wiki/guidelines/Q14]], before formal validation.

### Validation Tests and Performance Characteristics (Table 1)

| Test | Identity | Impurity (Quantitative) | Impurity (Limit) | Assay/Content/Potency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Specificity | + | + | + | + |
| Response (Calibration) | - | + | - | + |
| Lower Range Limit | - | QL | DL | - |
| Accuracy | - | + | - | + |
| Repeatability | - | + | - | + |
| Intermediate Precision | - | +(when required) | - | +(when required) |

### Reportable Range (Section 2.3, Table 2)
9. Confirmed by demonstrating acceptable response, accuracy, and precision.
10. Standard ranges:
    - Assay: 80-120% of declared content
    - Potency: Lowest spec -20% to highest spec +20%
    - Content Uniformity: 70-130% of declared content
    - Impurity: reporting threshold to 120% of spec acceptance criterion

### Stability-Indicating Properties (Section 2.4)
11. Must detect changes in relevant quality attributes during storage.
12. Demonstrated using samples with relevant degradation products: spiked samples, stressed samples, aged samples.

### Multivariate Procedures (Section 2.5 — New in R2)
13. Results determined through multivariate calibration model using multiple input variables (e.g., spectrum).
14. Development and validation in two phases: model development (calibration + internal testing) and model validation (independent samples).
15. Reference analytical procedure required — its performance should equal or exceed expected multivariate procedure performance.

### Specificity/Selectivity (Section 3.1)
16. Three approaches: absence of interference, orthogonal procedure comparison, technology-inherent justification.
17. Where one procedure insufficient, combination of two or more recommended.
18. For assay: demonstrate discrimination of analyte in presence of impurities and excipients (spiking studies).
19. For purity/impurity: demonstrate absence of interference through stressed/spiked samples.

### Range and Response (Section 3.2)
20. Linear response: minimum 5 concentrations across range. Evaluate by regression. Report correlation coefficient, y-intercept, slope, residuals plot.
21. Non-linear response (new in R2): acceptable for immunoassays, cell-based assays (e.g., 4-/5-parameter logistic). Linearity of concentration-response curve not required — proportionality is.
22. Multivariate calibration (new in R2): linear or non-linear algorithms acceptable.

### Lower Range Limits (Section 3.2.3)
23. Detection Limit (DL): DL = 3.3σ/S
24. Quantitation Limit (QL): QL = 10σ/S
    Where σ = standard deviation of response, S = slope of calibration curve.
25. Methods: visual evaluation, signal-to-noise (DL: 3:1, QL: 10:1), standard deviation of linear response.

### Accuracy (Section 3.3.1)
26. Approaches: reference material comparison, spiking study, orthogonal procedure comparison.
27. Expressed as percent recovery or difference between found and known values.

### Precision (Section 3.3.2)
28. Repeatability: same operating conditions, short time interval.
29. Intermediate precision: within-laboratory variations (different days, analysts, equipment).
30. Reproducibility: between different laboratories (collaborative studies).
31. Expressed as standard deviation, relative standard deviation, or confidence interval.

### Robustness (Section 3.4)
32. Evaluated during development per [[wiki/guidelines/Q14]]. Demonstrates reliability under small, deliberate parameter variations.

### Lifecycle Validation (Section 2.2 — New in R2)
33. Changes during lifecycle may require partial or full revalidation. Risk-based justification for scope.
34. Transfer: partial/full revalidation and/or comparative analysis of representative samples.
35. Co-validation across multiple sites can satisfy transfer requirements.
Q2-R2.definitions:
- **Specificity**: Ability to assess unequivocally the analyte in the presence of components expected to be present.
- **Selectivity**: Ability to provide results attributable to the analyte in the presence of other components but not necessarily fully discriminating.
- **Accuracy**: Closeness of agreement between accepted reference value and measured value.
- **Precision**: Closeness of agreement between independent test results under prescribed conditions. Includes repeatability, intermediate precision, reproducibility.
- **Detection Limit (DL)**: Lowest amount of analyte that can be detected but not necessarily quantitated.
- **Quantitation Limit (QL)**: Lowest amount of analyte that can be quantitatively determined with suitable precision and accuracy.
- **Reportable Range**: Interval between lowest and highest results for which the analytical procedure provides suitable response, accuracy, and precision.
- **Robustness**: Capacity to remain unaffected by small, deliberate variations in method parameters.
- **Linearity**: Ability to obtain test results that are directly proportional to the concentration of analyte in the sample within a given range.
Q2-R2.contradictions:
- R2 is a complete rewrite of Q2(R1). The original Q2A/Q2B structure (text + methodology) is merged into a single document with significant expansion.
- Multivariate and non-linear response sections are entirely new — industry is still building experience on application.
- Alignment with Q14 means development and validation are now explicitly linked — development data can be used as part of validation.

Q3A.title: Impurities in New Drug Substances
Q3A.summary: ICH Q3A(R2) provides guidance on the classification, identification, reporting, and qualification of impurities in new drug substances produced by chemical synthesis. It establishes thresholds for reporting, identification, and qualification of organic impurities.
Q3A.scope: New drug substances produced by chemical synthesis for registration applications. Excludes: biological/biotechnological, peptide, oligonucleotide, radiopharmaceutical, fermentation products, herbal products, crude animal/plant origin products. Not intended for clinical research stage.
Q3A.key_requirements:
### Classification
1. Three categories: organic impurities (process- and drug-related), inorganic impurities, residual solvents (per [[wiki/guidelines/Q3C]]).
2. Organic impurities include: starting materials, by-products, intermediates, degradation products, reagents, ligands, catalysts.

### Thresholds (Attachment 1)
3. **Reporting threshold**: Depends on maximum daily dose (MDD).
   - MDD ≤ 2g/day: 0.05%
   - MDD > 2g/day: 0.03%
4. **Identification threshold**:
   - MDD ≤ 2g/day: 0.10% or 1.0 mg/day intake (whichever is lower)
   - MDD > 2g/day: 0.05%
5. **Qualification threshold**:
   - MDD ≤ 2g/day: 0.15% or 1.0 mg/day intake (whichever is lower)
   - MDD > 2g/day: 0.05%

### Analytical Procedures
6. Validated, stability-indicating procedures required. Specificity demonstrated.
7. Impurity content reported by validated procedure; results expressed in context of the drug substance.

### Reporting
8. All impurities at or above reporting threshold should be reported.
9. Report should include batch analysis results with identified and unidentified impurities.

### Specification
10. List identified and unidentified impurities individually in specification.
11. Total impurities acceptance criterion should be set.

### Qualification
12. Impurities present at levels above qualification threshold require qualification through safety studies.
13. Qualification via literature, toxicological studies, or scientific arguments.
14. Decision tree provided (Attachment 3) for identification and qualification.
Q3A.definitions:
- **Impurity**: Any component of the drug substance that is not the chemical entity defined as the drug substance.
- **Identified impurity**: Impurity for which a structural characterization has been achieved.
- **Unidentified impurity**: Impurity defined solely by qualitative analytical properties (e.g., chromatographic retention time).
- **Specified impurity**: Identified or unidentified impurity selected for inclusion in specification.
- **Qualification**: Process of acquiring and evaluating data establishing biological safety of an individual impurity or a given impurity profile at the levels specified.
Q3A.contradictions:
- Excludes polymorphic forms and enantiomeric impurities — these are addressed elsewhere.
- Thresholds are based on maximum daily dose; for products with variable dosing, threshold determination can be complex.

Q3B.title: Impurities in New Drug Products
Q3B.summary: ICH Q3B(R2) provides guidance on the reporting, identification, and qualification of degradation products in new drug products produced from chemically synthesized drug substances. Complementary to [[wiki/guidelines/Q3A]].
Q3B.scope: Degradation products of drug substance and reaction products of drug substance with excipients and/or container closure system. Excludes: impurities from excipients, extractables/leachables (see [[wiki/guidelines/Q3E]]), same product categories as Q3A.
Q3B.key_requirements:
### Thresholds (Attachment 1)
1. **Reporting threshold**: Based on MDD.
   - MDD ≤ 1g: 0.1%
   - MDD > 1g: 0.05%
2. **Identification threshold**: Based on MDD.
   - MDD ≤ 1mg: 1.0% or 5 μg TDI (whichever is lower)
   - MDD 1mg-10mg: 0.5% or 20 μg TDI (whichever is lower)
   - MDD 10mg-2g: 0.2% or 2 mg TDI (whichever is lower)
   - MDD > 2g: 0.10%
3. **Qualification threshold**: Based on MDD.
   - MDD ≤ 1mg: 1.0% or 50 μg TDI (whichever is lower)
   - MDD 1mg-10mg: 0.5% or 200 μg TDI (whichever is lower)
   - MDD 10mg-100mg: 0.2% or 3 mg TDI (whichever is lower)
   - MDD 100mg-2g: 0.2%
   - MDD > 2g: 0.10%

### Analytical Procedures
4. Validated, specificity demonstrated under stress conditions (light, heat, humidity, acid/base, oxidation).
5. Quantitation limit not more than reporting threshold.

### Reporting and Specification
6. All degradation products at or above reporting threshold reported.
7. Decision tree (Attachment 3) for identification and qualification of degradation products.
Q3B.definitions: - **Degradation product**: Impurity resulting from chemical change in drug substance during manufacture and/or storage of drug product.
Q3B.contradictions:
- Drug substance impurities need not be monitored in drug product unless they are also degradation products (per Q6A).
- Technical factors may justify alternative thresholds (e.g., low drug substance to excipient ratio).

Q3C.title: Impurities: Guideline for Residual Solvents
Q3C.summary: ICH Q3C provides guidance on residual solvents in drug substances, excipients, and drug products. Solvents are classified by toxicity risk (Class 1-3) with Permitted Daily Exposures (PDEs) established for each. Regularly revised to add/update solvent entries — R9 is the current version.
Q3C.scope: Residual solvents in drug substances, excipients, and drug products. Addresses solvents used during manufacturing that are not completely removed.
Q3C.key_requirements:
### Solvent Classification
1. **Class 1 — Should be avoided**: Known human carcinogens or environmental hazards. Examples: benzene (PDE 2 ppm), carbon tetrachloride, 1,2-dichloroethane.
2. **Class 2 — Should be limited**: Non-genotoxic animal carcinogens or possible causative agents of irreversible toxicity. Examples: acetonitrile (PDE 410 ppm), chloroform (60 ppm), dichloromethane (600 ppm), DMF (880 ppm), methanol (3000 ppm), toluene (890 ppm).
3. **Class 3 — Low toxic potential**: No health-based exposure limit needed. PDE of 50 mg/day or 5000 ppm acceptable. Examples: acetone, ethanol, ethyl acetate, isopropanol, MTBE.

### Testing Approach
3. Option 1: apply limits per 3.2 (PDE-based).
4. Option 2: apply concentration limits per Table 2 (assume 10g daily dose).
5. Loss on drying can substitute for specific testing if Class 1 and Class 2 solvents are not used.
6. If only Class 3 solvents used: loss on drying test (0.5%) is acceptable without specific solvent identification.

### Analytical Methods
7. GC with appropriate detection (FID or MS) is the standard method.
8. Validated per [[wiki/guidelines/Q2-R2]].
Q3C.contradictions:
- Regularly revised (R1 through R9) to add new solvent monographs — check for the latest revision.
- R10 concept paper published for further updates.

Q3D.title: Guideline for Elemental Impurities
Q3D.summary: ICH Q3D(R2) establishes Permitted Daily Exposures (PDEs) for elemental impurities in drug products and provides a risk-based approach to controlling them. R2 (2022) added corrections to PDEs for gold, silver, nickel and added Appendix 5 for cutaneous/transcutaneous route.
Q3D.scope: Applies to new and existing drug products and drug substances used in drug products. Covers elements classified by toxicity and likelihood of occurrence. Four routes of administration: oral, parenteral, inhalation, cutaneous/transcutaneous (new in R2).
Q3D.key_requirements:
### Element Classification
1. **Class 1**: Elements with significant toxicity. Should be evaluated in all drug products. Includes: As, Cd, Hg, Pb.
2. **Class 2A**: Elements with high probability of occurring. Should be evaluated across all routes. Includes: Co, Ni, V.
3. **Class 2B**: Elements with lower probability of occurring. Risk assessment determines if evaluation needed. Includes: Ag, Au, Ir, Os, Pd, Pt, Rh, Ru, Se, Tl.
4. **Class 3**: Elements with low toxicity by oral route but requiring consideration by inhalation/parenteral. Includes: Ba, Cr, Cu, Li, Mo, Sb, Sn.

### Risk Assessment Approach
5. Identify and evaluate potential sources: drug substance synthesis (catalysts, reagents), excipients, manufacturing equipment, container closure system, water.
6. Risk assessment should consider intentionally added elements, elements likely to be present, and naturally occurring elements.
7. If total elemental impurity from all sources < 30% of PDE, no further action needed (Option 1).
8. Periodic verification recommended.

### PDEs by Route
9. PDEs established for 24 elements across oral, parenteral, inhalation, and cutaneous/transcutaneous routes.
10. Conversion factors provided to calculate concentration limits from PDEs based on daily dose.
Q3D.definitions:
- **Permitted Daily Exposure (PDE)**: Maximum acceptable intake of an elemental impurity per day.
- **Elemental impurity**: Any element present as impurity in drug substance, excipient, water, or arising from manufacturing equipment or container closure system.
Q3D.contradictions:
- R2 added cutaneous/transcutaneous route PDEs — industry adoption still maturing.
- Large parenterals (LVPs) may have different considerations due to volume administered.
- Risk assessment approach gives flexibility but can lead to inconsistent application.

Q3E.title: Extractables and Leachables
Q3E.summary: ICH Q3E provides guidance on the assessment and control of extractables and leachables (E&L) from packaging, manufacturing equipment, and delivery systems in drug products. Step 2 draft issued July 2025 — not yet finalized.
Q3E.scope: Drug products in all dosage forms. Covers primary packaging components, secondary packaging (when relevant), manufacturing process equipment, drug delivery device components. Addresses both extractables (released under exaggerated conditions) and leachables (released under normal conditions of use/storage).
Q3E.key_requirements:
1. Risk-based approach to determine need for E&L assessment.
2. Extraction studies using appropriate solvents and conditions.
3. Analytical evaluation threshold (AET) based on safety-derived Threshold of Toxicological Concern (TTC).
4. Identification and qualification of leachables above thresholds.
5. Routine monitoring of leachables during stability studies where appropriate.
6. Class 3 leachable monographs provided in supporting documentation.
Q3E.contradictions:
- Step 2 draft — content may change before final adoption.
- Interaction with existing regional E&L guidance (e.g., FDA guidance on container closure systems).

Q4B.title: Evaluation and Recommendation of Pharmacopoeial Texts for Use in the ICH Regions
Q4B.summary: ICH Q4B provides a framework for evaluating pharmacopoeial general chapter texts (from EP, JP, USP) to determine their interchangeability. When texts are deemed interchangeable, a manufacturer can use any of the three pharmacopoeial methods for a given test, regardless of the region of registration. 14 Annexes cover specific test procedures.
Q4B.scope: General chapter pharmacopoeial texts for test procedures used in quality control. Does not cover monographs. Covers tests like dissolution, disintegration, sterility, bacterial endotoxins, microbial limits, uniformity of dosage units, etc.
Q4B.key_requirements:
1. Pharmacopoeial texts evaluated for interchangeability across EP, JP, USP.
2. If interchangeable, any of the three texts can be used in any ICH region for registration.
3. 14 Annexes provide specific evaluations for individual test procedures.
4. Q4B FAQs provide additional clarification on implementation.

### Annexes Covered
- Annex 1: Residue on Ignition / Sulphated Ash
- Annex 2: Test for Extractable Volume of Parenteral Preparations
- Annex 3: Test for Particulate Contamination (Sub-Visible Particles)
- Annex 4A/4B/4C: Microbiological Examination of Non-Sterile Products
- Annex 5: Disintegration Test
- Annex 6: Uniformity of Dosage Units
- Annex 7: Dissolution Test
- Annex 8: Sterility Test
- Annex 9: Tablet Friability
- Annex 10: Polyacrylamide Gel Electrophoresis
- Annex 11: Capillary Electrophoresis
- Annex 12: Analytical Sieving
- Annex 13: Peptide Mapping
- Annex 14: Bacterial Endotoxins Test

Q5A.title: Viral Safety Evaluation of Biotechnology Products Derived from Cell Lines of Human or Animal Origin
Q5A.summary: ICH Q5A(R2) provides guidance on viral safety evaluation for biotechnology products derived from characterized cell lines of human or animal origin. It covers a three-pronged complementary approach: selecting and testing cell lines, evaluating capacity of production to clear viruses, and testing product at appropriate steps. R2 (2023) updates the original 1999 guideline.
Q5A.scope: Products derived from characterized cell lines (CHO, NS0, hybridoma, etc.) of human or animal origin. Does not cover products derived from human blood/plasma or tissues, or live viral vaccines.
Q5A.key_requirements:
1. **Cell line characterization**: Test master cell bank (MCB) and working cell bank (WCB) for adventitious agents and endogenous retroviruses/retrovirus-like particles.
2. **Virus clearance studies**: Evaluate manufacturing process capacity to remove/inactivate viruses. Use model viruses representing a range of physico-chemical properties. Minimum two effective orthogonal clearance steps.
3. **Product testing**: Test unprocessed bulk harvest for adventitious viruses. Test purified bulk for relevant viruses.
4. **Risk assessment approach** (enhanced in R2): Evaluate overall viral safety using principles from [[wiki/guidelines/Q9-R1]].
5. **Action plan for detected virus**: Define steps if an adventitious virus is detected during production.
6. **Raw materials**: Assess viral safety risk of animal-derived raw materials (e.g., serum, trypsin).
Q5A.contradictions:
- R2 significantly modernizes the approach with risk assessment and allows more flexibility in testing strategies.
- Applicability to novel cell substrates (insect cells, plant cells) may require case-by-case consideration.

Q5B.title: Quality of Biotechnological Products: Analysis of the Expression Construct in Cells Used for Production
Q5B.summary: ICH Q5B provides guidance on the analysis and characterization of the expression construct (gene, vector, host cell) used to produce recombinant DNA-derived protein products. It ensures the genetic stability and integrity of the production system.
Q5B.scope: Recombinant DNA-derived protein products where a well-characterized expression system is used.
Q5B.key_requirements:
1. Characterize the expression construct: cloned gene, expression vector, host cell.
2. Demonstrate genetic stability of the construct through production cell culture.
3. Analyze at the level of the MCB and at or beyond the limit of in vitro cell age (production end).
4. Restriction enzyme mapping, DNA sequencing of the cloned gene, copy number determination.
Q5B.contradictions: None identified. Brief guideline — largely incorporated into broader regulatory practice.

Q5C.title: Quality of Biotechnological Products: Stability Testing of Biotechnological/Biological Products
Q5C.summary: ICH Q5C provides stability testing guidance specific to biotechnological/biological products (e.g., proteins, polypeptides derived from rDNA, hybridoma, etc.). It adapts [[wiki/guidelines/Q1A]] principles for the unique degradation characteristics of biological molecules.
Q5C.scope: Well-characterized proteins and polypeptides, their derivatives, and products of which they are components, isolated from tissues/body fluids/cell culture/rDNA technology. Excludes antibiotics, allergenic extracts, heparins, vitamins, whole blood/blood components.
Q5C.key_requirements:
1. Real-time/real-condition studies primary basis for shelf life. Accelerated/stress studies to support, but limited predictive value for biologics.
2. At least 3 batches for which manufacturing is representative of production scale. Pilot-scale acceptable with justification.
3. Tests include: potency (bioassay or biochemical), purity/molecular characterization (SDS-PAGE, HPLC, etc.), sterility, moisture content, pH, visual appearance. Additional product-specific tests as appropriate.
4. Container closure integrity critical — biologics often in liquid formulations.
5. Reconstituted/diluted products: in-use stability data required.
6. Photostability may apply per [[wiki/guidelines/Q1B]].
7. Shelf life typically established with expiry date, not retest date (biologics are generally too labile for re-testing).
Q5C.contradictions:
- Accelerated and stress testing has limited predictive value for proteins — different from small molecules.
- Statistical extrapolation approaches in [[wiki/guidelines/Q1E]] may not directly apply.

Q5D.title: Derivation and Characterisation of Cell Substrates Used for Production of Biotechnological/Biological Products
Q5D.summary: ICH Q5D provides guidance on the derivation, characterization, and maintenance of cell substrates (cell banks) used for production of biotechnological/biological products. Covers MCB, WCB, and end-of-production cells.
Q5D.scope: Cell substrates including microbial cells, insect cells, avian cells, and mammalian cells used for production of biotechnological products.
Q5D.key_requirements:
1. **Cell bank system**: Establish MCB → WCB → production cells. Document history and provenance.
2. **Characterization**: Identity, purity (freedom from adventitious agents), genetic stability, tumorigenicity (where applicable).
3. **Storage and maintenance**: Defined conditions to maintain viability and prevent contamination. Access controls.
4. **Testing at MCB, WCB, and end-of-production**: Sterility, mycoplasma, adventitious viruses, retrovirus testing, identity, karyology.
5. **Documentation**: Complete history from tissue source through cell banking, including all manipulations and passage history.

Q5E.title: Comparability of Biotechnological/Biological Products Subject to Changes in Their Manufacturing Process
Q5E.summary: ICH Q5E provides principles for assessing comparability of biotechnological/biological products before and after changes in their manufacturing process. It defines a stepwise approach: analytical, then functional, then nonclinical, then clinical comparability studies as needed.
Q5E.scope: Well-characterized biotechnological/biological products subject to manufacturing process changes during development or post-approval.
Q5E.key_requirements:
1. **Comparability exercise**: Demonstrate that pre- and post-change product has highly similar quality attributes, and that existing knowledge is sufficient to predict that any differences have no adverse impact on safety or efficacy.
2. **Stepwise approach**: Start with analytical/physicochemical comparison. If these show high similarity, functional studies may suffice. Nonclinical and clinical studies only if analytical/functional are insufficient.
3. **Quality attributes to compare**: Physicochemical properties, biological activity, immunochemical properties, purity, impurity profiles, quantity.
4. **Stability**: Side-by-side stability comparison of pre- and post-change product.
5. **Applies to**: Scale-up, site transfer, process changes, formulation changes, changes in container closure system.
6. Comparability is not identity — minor differences may be acceptable if they do not affect safety/efficacy.
Q5E.contradictions:
- "Highly similar" is not precisely defined — relies on totality of evidence.
- Relationship to biosimilar assessment (different regulatory pathway) can cause confusion.

Q6A.title: Specifications: Test Procedures and Acceptance Criteria for New Drug Substances and New Drug Products: Chemical Substances
Q6A.summary: ICH Q6A provides guidance on setting and justifying specifications (tests, procedures, acceptance criteria) for new drug substances and drug products that are chemical entities. It establishes a framework for universal and specific tests, and introduces decision trees for common specification decisions.
Q6A.scope: New drug substances and drug products containing chemically synthesized new molecular entities. Excludes biotech/biological products (see [[wiki/guidelines/Q6B]]).
Q6A.key_requirements:
### Drug Substance Specifications
1. **Universal tests** (required in all cases): description, identification, assay, impurities.
2. **Specific tests** (where applicable): physicochemical properties (polymorphism, particle size, water content, residual solvents per [[wiki/guidelines/Q3C]], inorganic impurities), chiral purity, microbial limits.

### Drug Product Specifications
3. **Universal tests**: description, identification, assay, impurities (degradation products per [[wiki/guidelines/Q3B]]).
4. **Specific tests**: dissolution/disintegration, hardness/friability, uniformity of dosage units, water content, microbial limits, antimicrobial preservative content, antioxidant preservative content, extractables, functionality tests (dose delivery), etc.
5. Release vs. shelf life specifications may differ with justification.

### Decision Trees
6. Nine decision trees provided for common specification setting questions (e.g., when to set chirality test, dissolution test, microbial limits, particle size for drug substance).

### Periodic/Skip Testing
7. Certain tests may be performed periodically rather than on every batch if justified.

Q6B.title: Specifications: Test Procedures and Acceptance Criteria for Biotechnological/Biological Products
Q6B.summary: ICH Q6B provides guidance on setting specifications for biotechnological and biological products. Covers characterization, specification setting, process-related and product-related impurities, and reference standards for these complex molecules.
Q6B.scope: Well-characterized proteins and polypeptides, their derivatives, and products of which they are components. Same scope as [[wiki/guidelines/Q5C]].
Q6B.key_requirements:
1. Extensive characterization required: primary/secondary/higher-order structure, biological activity, immunochemical properties, purity/impurities.
2. Specification tests: appearance, identity, purity, potency (biological activity), quantity, sterility, endotoxin, general tests.
3. Process-related impurities: host cell proteins, host cell DNA, cell culture media components, downstream processing reagents.
4. Product-related impurities: molecular variants (truncated forms, aggregates, deamidated, oxidized, etc.).
5. Reference standards: well-characterized, qualified in-house reference standard required. Calibrated against international/national standards where available.
6. Justification of acceptance criteria based on manufacturing experience, stability data, preclinical/clinical data.

Q7.title: Good Manufacturing Practice Guide for Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients
Q7.summary: ICH Q7 is the definitive GMP guideline for manufacturing APIs intended for use in human drug products. It covers the entire lifecycle from receipt of materials through production, packaging, quality control, release, storage, and distribution of APIs. It is the baseline standard against which API manufacturers are audited worldwide.
Q7.scope:
Applies to manufacture of APIs for human drug products. Covers chemical synthesis, extraction, cell culture/fermentation, recovery from natural sources. Excludes vaccines, whole cells, whole blood/plasma, plasma derivatives, gene therapy APIs, medical gases, radiopharmaceuticals. Sterile APIs covered only up to the point prior to sterilization. Section 18 provides specific guidance for cell culture/fermentation. Section 19 covers APIs for clinical trials.

GMP stringency increases as the process progresses from early steps to final purification and packaging (Table 1 in guideline).
Q7.key_requirements:
### Quality Management (Section 2)
1. Quality is the responsibility of all persons involved in manufacturing.
2. Independent quality unit(s) required — separate from production.
3. Quality unit responsibilities include: releasing/rejecting APIs, reviewing batch records, investigating deviations, approving specs/procedures/changes, approving contract manufacturers, ensuring stability data supports retest/expiry dates.
4. Annual Product Quality Review (PQR/APQR) required covering: critical test results, failed batches, deviations, changes, stability monitoring, complaints, recalls, corrective actions.
5. All quality activities recorded at time of performance.
6. Deviations documented and explained; critical deviations investigated.

### Personnel (Section 3)
7. Adequate qualified personnel. Responsibilities in writing.
8. Regular GMP training, documented and periodically assessed.
9. Personnel hygiene: clean clothing, no direct contact with APIs, restricted eating/smoking areas.

### Buildings and Facilities (Section 4)
10. Designed to facilitate cleaning, minimize contamination.
11. Adequate space to prevent mix-ups.
12. Dedicated production areas for highly sensitizing materials (e.g. penicillins, cephalosporins) or highly potent/toxic materials. Campaign production in same facilities acceptable with validated cleaning for certain agents.
13. Water: minimum potable for process use; purified water meeting specified specs when needed.

### Process Equipment (Section 5)
14. Equipment of appropriate design, adequate size, suitable material (non-reactive, non-additive, non-absorptive).
15. Written SOPs for cleaning and maintenance. Equipment cleaning records.
16. Calibration per written procedures; traceable to certified standards (NIST or equivalent).
17. Computerized systems: validated, documented, controlled access, change control, data backup.

### Documentation and Records (Section 6)
18. Written documentation system for all specs, procedures, records.
19. Master production instructions for each API/intermediate.
20. Batch production records for each batch — complete record enabling traceability.
21. Laboratory control records maintained.
22. Batch production record review by QU before release.

### Materials Management (Section 7)
23. Written procedures for receipt, identification, quarantine, storage, handling, sampling, testing, approval/rejection.
24. Full testing or CoA + identity test for each incoming raw material.
25. Re-evaluation of stored materials at defined intervals.

### Production and In-Process Controls (Section 8)
26. Written production instructions.
27. Time limits for intermediates defined where quality could be impacted.
28. In-process controls and tests at critical points.
29. Blending: confined to combining batches of same specification; each batch must meet spec individually before blending.
30. Contamination control: dedicated or validated cleaning between campaigns.

### Packaging and Labeling (Section 9)
31. Written procedures. Physical separation during operations.
32. Labels: name/code, batch number, storage conditions, expiry/retest date.

### Storage and Distribution (Section 10)
33. Appropriate storage conditions (temperature, humidity where critical).
34. Segregation of quarantined, rejected, returned, recalled materials.
35. Distribution traceability for recall capability.

### Laboratory Controls (Section 11)
36. Scientifically sound specs, sampling plans, test procedures.
37. Impurity profile established for each API.
38. OOS results investigated per written procedure.
39. Reference standards: primary from official sources where possible; secondary qualified against primary.
40. Stability monitoring program: first 3 commercial batches, then at least 1 per year. Consistent with ICH guidelines ([[wiki/guidelines/Q1A]]).
41. Certificates of Analysis: authentic, complete, signed by QU.
42. Reserve samples retained: 1 year after expiry OR 3 years after distribution (whichever longer).

### Validation (Section 12)
43. Validation policy documented.
44. Qualification: DQ → IQ → OQ → PQ.
45. Process validation: 3 consecutive successful batches (prospective). Concurrent and retrospective approaches defined.
46. Cleaning validation required. Residue limits based on potency, toxicity, stability. Swab and/or rinse sampling.
47. Analytical method validation per ICH guidelines ([[wiki/guidelines/Q2-R2]]).
48. Periodic review of validated systems.

### Change Control (Section 13)
49. Formal change control system for all changes that may affect API quality.
50. Changes classified by potential impact. Evaluated for testing/validation needs.
51. QU review and approval required.
52. Impact on retest/expiry dates evaluated. First batches under change evaluated.
53. Current dosage form manufacturers notified of changes.

### Rejection and Re-Use (Section 14)
54. Reprocessing: repeating established process steps — generally acceptable.
55. Reworking: using different process steps — requires investigation, evaluation, testing, and stability assessment.
56. Solvent recovery: controlled, monitored, tested before reuse.
57. Returns: quarantined, evaluated, documented.

### Complaints and Recalls (Section 15)
58. Written procedures for complaint investigation and recall.
59. Complaint records maintained with full details.
60. Recall procedure defined. Serious situations reported to authorities.

### Contract Manufacturing (Section 16)
61. Contract manufacturers must comply with Q7.
62. Written contract defining GMP responsibilities.
63. Contract giver retains right to audit.

### Agents, Brokers, Traders, Distributors (Section 17)
64. Must comply with Q7.
65. Complete traceability maintained.
66. Repackaging stability studies required if different container type.

### Cell Culture/Fermentation (Section 18)
67. Additional controls for biotech and classical fermentation.
68. Cell bank maintenance, access control, viability monitoring.
69. Critical operating parameters monitored (temp, pH, agitation, gases).
70. Viral removal/inactivation steps validated ([[wiki/guidelines/Q5A]]).
71. Bioburden and endotoxin monitoring at appropriate stages.

### APIs for Clinical Trials (Section 19)
72. Controls consistent with development stage — flexibility allowed.
73. Independent quality unit still required.
74. Process validation not normally required for single batches.
75. Analytical methods scientifically sound but need not be validated.
76. All changes documented.
Q7.definitions:
- **API Starting Material**: Raw material, intermediate, or API used in production and incorporated as significant structural fragment. Company designates the point at which GMP begins.
- **Reprocessing**: Repeating established process steps (crystallization, distillation, filtration, milling). Acceptable if not for majority of batches.
- **Reworking**: Using steps different from established process. Requires investigation and concurrent validation.
- **Mother liquor**: Residual liquid after crystallization/isolation. Can be reused with approved procedures.
- **Impurity profile**: Description of identified and unidentified impurities present in a typical batch.
- **Qualification**: DQ, IQ, OQ, PQ — part of validation but not validation alone.
- **Quality unit**: Independent of production; fulfills both QA and QC responsibilities.
Q7.contradictions:
- Q7 uses "should" throughout — recommendations, not absolute requirements. Alternatives acceptable if equivalent quality assurance demonstrated.
- The point at which GMP begins for API production is left to the company to define and justify (API Starting Material designation).
- Q7 was adopted in 2000; some areas (computerized systems, data integrity) have been supplemented by subsequent regulatory guidance (EU Annex 11, FDA guidance on data integrity) that go beyond Q7's scope.

Q8-R2.title: Pharmaceutical Development
Q8-R2.summary: ICH Q8(R2) provides guidance on the contents of the Pharmaceutical Development section (3.2.P.2) of a regulatory submission. It introduces Quality by Design (QbD) concepts including Quality Target Product Profile (QTPP), Critical Quality Attributes (CQAs), design space, and control strategy. R2 (2009) added Annex on pharmaceutical development with enhanced approach.
Q8-R2.scope: Drug products of all types including immediate and modified release solid oral dosage forms, semi-solids, liquids, and parenteral products. Applies throughout development and lifecycle.
Q8-R2.key_requirements:
1. **Quality Target Product Profile (QTPP)**: Prospective summary of desired quality characteristics to ensure safety and efficacy.
2. **Critical Quality Attributes (CQAs)**: Physical, chemical, biological, or microbiological property that must be within appropriate limits to ensure desired product quality.
3. **Design Space**: Multidimensional combination of input variables and process parameters demonstrated to provide assurance of quality. Working within design space is NOT a change per [[wiki/guidelines/Q10]].
4. **Control Strategy**: Planned set of controls derived from product and process understanding.
5. **Risk-based approach**: Use [[wiki/guidelines/Q9-R1]] principles throughout development.
6. Development topics: drug substance properties, excipient selection, formulation development, overages, physicochemical/biological properties, manufacturing process development, container closure system, microbiological attributes, compatibility.
7. Enhanced approach (Annex): systematic, science-based development leading to greater process understanding and potentially more regulatory flexibility.
8. Traditional approach also acceptable but may result in less process understanding.
Q8-R2.contradictions:
- Enhanced (QbD) approach is encouraged but not mandated — creates two-tier regulatory pathway.
- Design space concept varies in regulatory acceptance across regions.

Q9-R1.title: Quality Risk Management
Q9-R1.summary: ICH Q9(R1) provides a systematic framework for quality risk management (QRM) across the pharmaceutical product lifecycle. It defines principles, processes, methodologies, and tools for assessing, controlling, communicating, and reviewing risks to drug substance and drug product quality. R1 (2023) added guidance on formality, risk-based decision-making, managing subjectivity, and product availability risks.
Q9-R1.scope: Applies to all aspects of pharmaceutical quality: development, manufacturing, distribution, inspection, and submission/review processes. Covers drug substances, drug products, biological and biotechnological products throughout the product lifecycle. Complements [[wiki/guidelines/Q10]] (PQS), [[wiki/guidelines/Q8-R2]] (development), and [[wiki/guidelines/Q12]] (lifecycle management).
Q9-R1.key_requirements:
### Two Primary Principles
1. Risk evaluation should be based on scientific knowledge and ultimately link to patient protection. Product availability risks from quality/manufacturing issues are included.
2. Level of effort, formality, and documentation should be commensurate with the level of risk.

### QRM Process (Figure 1)
3. Systematic process: Risk Assessment → Risk Control → Risk Communication → Risk Review.
4. Risk Assessment consists of: Hazard Identification → Risk Analysis → Risk Evaluation.
5. Three fundamental questions: What might go wrong? What is the likelihood? What are the consequences?
6. Risk Analysis: qualitative or quantitative linking of likelihood and severity. Detectability may also factor in.
7. Risk Control: reduce risk to acceptable level. Balance benefits, risks, resources. Check if new risks introduced.
8. Risk Communication: sharing information between decision makers and stakeholders at any stage.
9. Risk Review: ongoing. Frequency based on level of risk. Triggered by planned events (reviews, audits, change control) or unplanned events (failures, recalls).

### Formality (New in R1 — Section 5.1)
10. Formality is a continuum, not binary (formal/informal).
11. Three factors determine formality level: uncertainty, importance, complexity.
12. Higher uncertainty/importance/complexity → more formal approach needed.
13. Resource constraints should not justify lower formality.
14. Higher formality characteristics: all QRM steps explicit, tools used, cross-functional team, facilitator, standalone reports.
15. Lower formality characteristics: QRM embedded within quality system, tools may not be used, standalone reports may not be generated.

### Risk-Based Decision-Making (New in R1 — Section 5.2)
16. Three approaches: highly structured (high importance + high uncertainty/complexity), less structured (high importance + lower uncertainty), rule-based/standardized (SOPs with predetermined actions).
17. Data integrity must be ensured for risk-based decision-making.

### Managing Subjectivity (New in R1 — Section 5.3)
18. Subjectivity cannot be eliminated but should be controlled.
19. Sources: differences in perception, bias, poorly defined risk questions, poorly designed scoring scales.
20. All participants should acknowledge, anticipate, and address subjectivity.

### Product Availability (New in R1 — Section 6.1)
21. Quality/manufacturing issues are a significant cause of product shortages.
22. QRM enables proactive identification of preventive measures.
23. Factors affecting supply: manufacturing process variability, facility/equipment robustness, oversight of outsourced activities.

### QRM Tools (Annex I)
24. Basic: flowcharts, check sheets, process mapping, Ishikawa/fishbone diagrams.
25. FMEA: evaluation of potential failure modes and their effects.
26. FMECA: FMEA extended with severity, probability, detectability → Risk Priority Number (RPN).
27. FTA (Fault Tree Analysis): top-down, assumes failure, identifies causal chains with logical operators.
28. HACCP: 7-step systematic approach for process hazard control.
29. HAZOP: systematic team-based approach using guide words for deviation analysis.
30. PHA: early lifecycle hazard identification.
31. Risk Ranking and Filtering: comparing and ranking risks using multiple quantitative/qualitative factors.
32. Statistical tools: control charts, DOE, histograms, Pareto charts, process capability analysis.

### Application Areas (Annex II)
33. Quality management, regulatory operations, development, facilities/equipment/utilities, materials management, production, lab control and stability, packaging and labeling, supply chain control.
Q9-R1.definitions:
- **Risk**: Combination of probability of occurrence of harm and severity of that harm.
- **Hazard**: Potential source of harm.
- **Harm**: Damage to health, including from loss of product quality or availability.
- **Detectability**: Ability to discover or determine existence/presence of a hazard.
- **Quality Risk Management**: Systematic process for assessment, control, communication and review of risks to quality across the product lifecycle.
- **Risk Assessment**: Systematic process of organizing information — hazard identification + risk analysis + risk evaluation.
- **Risk-Based Decision-Making**: Approach considering knowledge about risks and whether they are at acceptable level.
- **Severity**: Measure of possible consequences of a hazard.
- **Stakeholder**: Any individual/group/organization that can affect, be affected by, or perceive itself affected by a risk.
Q9-R1.contradictions:
- Q9(R1) explicitly states QRM should not be used to justify practices that would otherwise be unacceptable per regulations.
- The R1 revision significantly expanded guidance on formality and subjectivity — areas that were previously left open and led to inconsistent application across industry.
- Product availability as a quality risk is a notable expansion from original Q9 (which focused primarily on product quality risk).

change-control.title: Change Control
change-control.summary: Change control is a formal system to evaluate all changes that may affect the production and control of APIs or intermediates. It ensures changes are identified, documented, reviewed, approved, and their impact assessed before implementation.
change-control.key_requirements:
1. Formal change control system required.
2. Written procedures for identification, documentation, review, and approval.
3. QU review and approval of all changes.
4. Impact on quality evaluated. Classification by severity (minor/major) to determine testing and validation needs.
5. All affected documents revised after implementation.
6. First batches under change evaluated.
7. Impact on retest/expiry dates assessed — accelerated stability or addition to monitoring program may be needed.
8. Dosage form manufacturers notified of API process changes.

quality-risk-management.title: Quality Risk Management
quality-risk-management.summary: Quality risk management (QRM) is a systematic process for assessing, controlling, communicating, and reviewing risks to drug product quality throughout the product lifecycle. It is one of the three enablers of an effective pharmaceutical quality system alongside knowledge management and management review.
quality-risk-management.key_requirements:
1. Process: Hazard Identification → Risk Analysis → Risk Evaluation → Risk Control → Risk Communication → Risk Review.
