2. Based on scientific knowledge, ultimately linked to patient protection.
3. Effort commensurate with level of risk.
4. Formality is a continuum — determined by uncertainty, importance, complexity.
5. Subjectivity must be acknowledged and minimized.
6. Primary tools: FMEA/FMECA, FTA, HACCP, HAZOP, PHA, Risk Ranking.

quality-unit.title: Quality Unit
quality-unit.summary: The quality unit is an organizational unit independent of production that fulfills both QA and QC responsibilities. It can be separate QA and QC units or a single individual/group, depending on organization size and structure.
quality-unit.key_requirements:
1. Independent of production.
2. Responsible for: releasing/rejecting APIs and intermediates, establishing release systems for raw materials, reviewing batch records of critical steps, investigating deviations, approving specifications and master production instructions, approving procedures affecting quality, ensuring internal audits, approving contract manufacturers, approving changes affecting quality, reviewing validation protocols/reports, ensuring complaint investigation, ensuring equipment calibration, ensuring proper testing, ensuring stability data supports retest/expiry dates, performing annual product quality reviews.
3. Main responsibilities cannot be delegated.
4. All responsibilities documented in writing.

significant-change.title: Significant Change Criteria
significant-change.summary: "Significant change" is the threshold that triggers additional testing at intermediate storage conditions when observed during accelerated stability studies. The definition differs between drug substances and drug products.
significant-change.key_requirements:
### Drug Substance
Significant change = failure to meet its specification.

### Drug Product
Significant change is defined as any of the following:
1. A 5% change in assay from its initial value; or failure to meet acceptance criteria for potency when using biological or immunological procedures.
2. Any degradation product exceeding its acceptance criterion.
3. Failure to meet acceptance criteria for appearance, physical attributes, and functionality test (e.g., color, phase separation, resuspendibility, caking, hardness, dose delivery per actuation). Note: some physical changes (e.g., softening of suppositories, melting of creams) may be expected under accelerated conditions.
4. Failure to meet the acceptance criterion for pH (as appropriate for dosage form).
5. Failure to meet the acceptance criteria for dissolution for 12 dosage units (as appropriate for dosage form).

### Semi-Permeable Containers — Water Loss
A 5% loss in water from initial value after equivalent of 3 months at 40°C/NMT 25% RH is considered significant change. For small containers (1 mL or less) or unit-dose products, a higher threshold may be acceptable if justified.

### Consequences of Significant Change
- If significant change occurs at accelerated condition: intermediate testing must be conducted.
- If significant change occurs between 3-6 months (refrigerated products): shelf life based on real-time long-term data only.
- If significant change occurs within first 3 months (refrigerated products): discussion of short-term excursion effects required; unnecessary to continue accelerated study to 6 months.

stability.title: Stability
stability.summary: Stability is the capacity of a drug substance or drug product to remain within established specifications over its re-test period or shelf life when stored under defined conditions. It is a foundational quality attribute that determines how long a product can be used safely and effectively.
stability.key_requirements:
1. Stability must be demonstrated through formal studies under long-term, accelerated, and (where applicable) intermediate conditions per [[wiki/guidelines/Q1A]].
2. Environmental factors assessed: temperature, humidity, light (photostability per [[wiki/guidelines/Q1B]]).
3. Attributes tested: physical, chemical, biological, microbiological as appropriate.
4. Minimum 3 primary batches required for formal studies.
5. Validated stability-indicating analytical procedures mandatory.
6. Statistical evaluation of data to establish re-test period or shelf life.

storage-conditions.title: Storage Conditions for Stability Testing
storage-conditions.summary: ICH Q1A(R2) defines specific temperature and humidity conditions for long-term, intermediate, and accelerated stability studies. Conditions vary by intended storage (room temp, refrigerated, freezer) and container type (general, impermeable, semi-permeable).
storage-conditions.key_requirements:
### General Case (Room Temperature Storage)
| Study | Condition | Min Duration |
|---|---|---|
| Long-term | 25°C ± 2°C / 60% RH ± 5% RH OR 30°C ± 2°C / 65% RH ± 5% RH | 12 months |
| Intermediate | 30°C ± 2°C / 65% RH ± 5% RH | 6 months |
| Accelerated | 40°C ± 2°C / 75% RH ± 5% RH | 6 months |

If 30°C/65% RH is long-term, no intermediate study needed.

### Refrigerated Storage
| Study | Condition | Min Duration |
|---|---|---|
| Long-term | 5°C ± 3°C | 12 months |
| Accelerated | 25°C ± 2°C / 60% RH ± 5% RH | 6 months |

### Freezer Storage
| Study | Condition | Min Duration |
|---|---|---|
| Long-term | -20°C ± 5°C | 12 months |

### Semi-Permeable Containers
| Study | Condition | Min Duration |
|---|---|---|
| Long-term | 25°C ± 2°C / 40% RH ± 5% RH OR 30°C ± 2°C / 35% RH ± 5% RH | 12 months |
| Intermediate | 30°C ± 2°C / 65% RH ± 5% RH | 6 months |
| Accelerated | 40°C ± 2°C / NMT 25% RH | 6 months |

### Water Loss Rate Ratios (Semi-Permeable)
| Alt RH | Ref RH | Ratio |
|---|---|---|
| 60% RH | 25% RH | 1.9 |
| 60% RH | 40% RH | 1.5 |
| 65% RH | 35% RH | 1.9 |
| 75% RH | 25% RH | 3.0 |

### Storage Condition Tolerances
Equipment must control conditions within defined ranges. Excursions beyond defined tolerances for more than 24 hours must be described and their effect assessed.

stress-testing.title: Stress Testing
stress-testing.summary: Stress testing (forced degradation) is conducted on a drug substance to identify likely degradation products, establish degradation pathways, determine intrinsic stability, and validate the stability-indicating power of analytical procedures. For drug products, it assesses the effect of severe conditions including photostability.
stress-testing.key_requirements:
1. Drug substance stress testing should include:
   - Temperature: 10°C increments above accelerated (e.g., 50°C, 60°C)
   - Humidity: 75% RH or greater where appropriate
   - Oxidation
   - Photolysis (per [[wiki/guidelines/Q1B]])
   - Hydrolysis across wide pH range (solution/suspension)
2. Conducted on a single batch.
3. Results help establish degradation pathways — not all degradation products found under stress need to be monitored if they are not formed under accelerated or long-term conditions.
4. Drug product stress testing includes photostability per [[wiki/guidelines/Q1B]] and dosage-form-specific tests (e.g., MDIs, creams, emulsions, refrigerated aqueous liquids).

validation.title: Validation
validation.summary: Validation is a documented program providing a high degree of assurance that a specific process, method, or system will consistently produce results meeting pre-determined acceptance criteria. For APIs, it encompasses process validation, cleaning validation, analytical method validation, and computerized system validation.
validation.key_requirements:
1. Qualification (DQ → IQ → OQ → PQ) must be completed before process validation.
2. Process validation: 3 consecutive successful production batches (prospective). Concurrent and retrospective approaches defined for specific circumstances.
3. Cleaning validation: residue limits based on potency, toxicity, stability. Swab and/or rinse sampling.
4. Analytical method validation per [[wiki/guidelines/Q2-R2]].
5. Computerized system validation required.
6. Periodic review of validated systems.
7. Validation protocols reviewed and approved by QU.
8. Validation reports cross-referencing protocols, summarizing results, documenting deviations.

Q11-QA.title: ICH Q11 Questions and Answers
Q11-QA.summary: Official ICH Q&A document providing clarifications on [[wiki/guidelines/Q11]] Drug Substance Development and Manufacture.
Q11-QA.key_requirements:
### Starting Material Selection
- Selection of starting material should be justified based on chemical transformation, quality controls, and regulatory considerations.
- The later the starting material is designated in the synthesis, the more GMP steps are required.

### Process Development
- Enhanced approach (QbD) is encouraged but not required.
- Design space for drug substance manufacturing follows same principles as drug product per [[wiki/guidelines/Q8-R2]].

### Control Strategy
- Drug substance control strategy should cover starting materials, intermediates, reagents, solvents, process parameters, and final drug substance specifications.
- Specification for drug substance should include: description, identification, assay, impurities (organic, inorganic, residual solvents), physicochemical properties.

Q12-Annexes.title: ICH Q12 Annexes — Examples of Established Conditions and PACMPs
Q12-Annexes.summary: Annexes to [[wiki/guidelines/Q12]] providing illustrative examples of Established Conditions (ECs) and Post-Approval Change Management Protocols (PACMPs) for drug substances and drug products.
Q12-Annexes.key_requirements:
### Annex I — Established Conditions Examples
- Drug substance: API starting materials, synthesis steps with defined ranges, critical in-process controls, final specification.
- Drug product: formulation composition, manufacturing process steps and parameters, container closure system specifications.

### Annex II — PACMP Examples
- Pre-approved protocol for anticipated changes (e.g., adding an alternative API supplier, changing batch size, modifying a manufacturing step within defined limits).
- Protocol specifies: scope of change, testing to be performed, acceptance criteria, reporting requirements.

### Annex III — Product Lifecycle Management Concepts
- Relationship between development knowledge, manufacturing experience, and regulatory commitments.
- Guidance on categorizing post-approval changes based on risk assessment.

Q7-QA.title: ICH Q7 Questions and Answers
Q7-QA.summary: Official ICH Q&A document providing clarifications on the application of [[wiki/guidelines/Q7]] GMP requirements for APIs. Addresses common implementation questions.
Q7-QA.key_requirements:
### Quality Management
- Independent quality unit can be a single person in small organizations, provided independence from production is maintained.
- Product Quality Review (APQR) should cover all batches manufactured in the review period.

### Personnel & Training
- Training should cover GMP as it relates to the employee's specific functions.
- Consultants' qualifications should be verified and documented.

### Process Equipment & Calibration
- Equipment shared between API and non-API manufacturing requires validated cleaning and risk assessment.
- Calibration programs should cover all critical instruments, with defined intervals based on equipment type and usage.

### Validation
- Process validation for legacy products can use retrospective approach with 10-30 consecutive batches.
- Cleaning validation representative product selection should consider solubility, difficulty of cleaning, and calculation of residue limits.

### Change Control
- All GMP-relevant changes require formal change control, but the level of evaluation can be risk-based.
- Changes to API starting material specifications should be evaluated for impact on API quality.

### Reprocessing vs. Reworking
- Continuation of a process step after in-process control shows incompleteness is normal process, not reprocessing.
- Reworking requires investigation, concurrent validation approach, and impurity profile comparison.

Q8Q9Q10-QA.title: ICH Q8, Q9, Q10 Questions and Answers (R5)
Q8Q9Q10-QA.summary: Official ICH Q&A document providing clarifications on the interrelated application of [[wiki/guidelines/Q8-R2]] (Pharmaceutical Development), [[wiki/guidelines/Q9-R1]] (Quality Risk Management), and [[wiki/guidelines/Q10]] (Pharmaceutical Quality System).
Q8Q9Q10-QA.key_requirements:
### Design Space
- Design space is proposed by applicant and assessed by regulators. It is part of the marketing authorization.
- Movement within design space is not a regulatory change but must be managed within the PQS change management system.
- Design space is specific to a manufacturing site unless scientifically justified otherwise.

### Quality Risk Management
- QRM should be applied throughout the product lifecycle, not just during development.
- The level of formality should be commensurate with the risk — not every decision requires a full FMEA.
- Risk assessment outcomes should be documented and traceable.

### Control Strategy
- Control strategy should evolve based on accumulated knowledge and manufacturing experience.
- Real-time release testing (RTRT) is an enhanced control strategy option.
- PAT is a tool within the control strategy, not a separate requirement.

### Knowledge Management
- Companies should establish systematic processes for managing product and process knowledge.
- Knowledge should flow from development through technology transfer to commercial manufacturing.

### Continual Improvement
- Annual Product Quality Review/Product Quality Review is a key input for continual improvement.
- CAPA effectiveness should be verified and documented.
